title: Logarithmic concavity of Schur polynomials
speaker: June Huh
abstract: Schur polynomials are the characters of finite-dimensional irreducible representations of the general linear group. We will discuss both continuous and discrete concavity property of Schur polynomials. There will be one theorem and eight conjectures. No background beyond basic representation theory will be necessary to enjoy the talk. Based on joint work with Jacob Matherne, Karola Mészáros, and Avery St. Dizier.

proof_stages.label: Schur polynomials, tableaux, and weight multiplicities
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces the question of Lorentzianity for Schur polynomials and recalls their determinant, generating-function, tableau, and GL_n-character interpretations, identifying Kostka numbers with weight-space dimensions.

proof_stages.label: Convex and symplectic asymptotics of Schur supports
proof_stages.summary: The Newton polytope of a Schur polynomial is described as a saturated generalized permutohedron and, via the coadjoint-orbit moment map, its Duistermaat–Heckman measure gives asymptotic log concavity of weight multiplicities.

proof_stages.label: Failure of full prelimit Littlewood–Richardson log concavity
proof_stages.summary: Okounkov’s stronger conjectural log concavity for Littlewood–Richardson coefficients is reviewed, its containment of the Kostka-number problem is explained, and known counterexamples motivate restricting attention to weight multiplicities and root directions.

proof_stages.label: Main theorem: normalized Schur polynomials are Lorentzian
proof_stages.summary: The main result is stated: Kostka numbers are log concave along every type-A root direction, equivalently via the stronger analytic statement that the factorial-normalized Schur polynomial has concave logarithm on the positive orthant because it is Lorentzian.

proof_stages.label: Lorentzian criterion and consequences
proof_stages.summary: Lorentzian polynomials are defined by saturated generalized-permutohedron support together with a one-positive-eigenvalue condition for all quadratic partial derivatives, and this stronger property is presented as a practical route to the discrete and continuous concavity conclusions.

proof_stages.label: Hodge-theoretic proof mechanism
proof_stages.summary: The proof is sketched as a construction of a degeneracy locus for a vector-bundle homomorphism, followed by intersection-theoretic computations and an application of Hodge theory on the resulting projective variety to obtain Lorentzianity.

proof_stages.label: Conjectural extensions and hidden geometry
proof_stages.summary: The speaker formulates broad conjectures that suitably normalized Schubert, skew Schur, Schur P, Grothendieck, key polynomials, and weight multiplicities for arbitrary integral SL_n weights should satisfy analogous Lorentzian or root-direction log-concavity properties.

examples.name: Semistandard tableaux for λ=(2,1,0) in three variables
examples.note: The three-box Young diagram is filled with 1,2,3 to show how Schur-polynomial coefficients count semistandard Young tableaux, including the coefficient 2 of x1 x2 x3.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker draws the shape for λ=(2,1,0), applies the semistandard row/column rules, lists the resulting monomials, and explains coefficients by counting fillings.

examples.name: Sliding μ=(4,2,1) to realize a Kostka number as a Littlewood–Richardson coefficient
examples.note: A particular partition is drawn and augmented by dotted boxes to make concrete the linear transformation embedding Kostka numbers into Littlewood–Richardson coefficients.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker takes μ=(4,2,1) and describes sliding its boxes horizontally to form a skew shape with no column overlaps, thereby running the Kostka-to-Littlewood–Richardson construction on it.

examples.name: Hypothetical degree-37 polynomial in 52 variables
examples.note: The large-polynomial thought experiment contrasts the difficulty of checking concavity of log f by Hessians with the relative tractability of the Lorentzian support-and-eigenvalue tests.
examples.engagement_reason: The degree-37 polynomial in 52 variables is only a hypothetical illustration of checking difficulty; no particular polynomial is computed or verified.
